label: Wed · Jul 22
standfirst: A memory-chip rebound powered the Nasdaq and S&P 500 higher, even as crude oil hit a five-week high on escalating US-Iran strikes and a Houthi blockade threat against Saudi Arabia.
marketsNote: Breadth was broad with small-caps leading; the 10-year Treasury yield hovered near 4.59% as oil's climb to a five-week high kept energy in focus.

lead.headline: Chip rebound powers Wall Street higher as oil hits a five-week high
body: Beaten-down semiconductor stocks staged a sharp rebound, lifting the tech-heavy Nasdaq 1.29% and the S&P 500 0.89% to fresh highs. Micron surged about 12% and SanDisk jumped roughly 14% on renewed memory-chip demand, while AMD added 8% as strong South Korean AI-export data reinforced the sector's momentum. The Dow rose 0.74% and the small-cap Russell 2000 outpaced with a 1.53% gain.

The rally came despite mounting geopolitical risk. Crude oil climbed about 2% to a five-week high — WTI settled at $85.15 and Brent at $91.05 — after a 10th straight night of US strikes on Iran and a Houthi threat to blockade Saudi Arabia. Investors also weighed President Trump's announcement of 50% tariffs on Canadian goods and awaited results from General Motors, Halliburton and 3M ahead of Big Tech earnings later in the week.

banking.text: Global investment-banking revenue surged to $61.4 billion in the first half of 2026, up 24% year over year, with JPMorgan retaining the top revenue spot and Goldman Sachs leading M&A advisory.

SpaceX's roughly $86 billion IPO generated about $500 million in fees split among banks including Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, alongside Cerebras' $6.4 billion listing and Alphabet's $85 billion buyback.
Citi reported a 45% jump in profit and its highest quarterly revenue in a decade, while Goldman Sachs and Wells Fargo both topped Wall Street profit expectations.

banking.lead: Morgan Stanley sees a record pipeline.
banking.text: CFO comments flagged that roughly 70% of the top 100 unicorns sit in Morgan Stanley's IPO pipeline, with dealmaking momentum that could make 2026 a record year for the franchise.
JPMorgan's investment-banking fees hit their highest level since 2021 on a wave of IPO and M&A activity, though executives cautioned that valuations and leverage look stretched.

watch.title: Big Tech earnings and the Fed
watch.lead: The week's real test arrives next.
watch.text: Alphabet and other mega-caps report later this week, and their AI-spending guidance will shape whether the chip-led rally holds. Investors are also positioning for the Fed's late-July FOMC meeting, watching whether escalating oil prices complicate the rate path.

world.text: American forces struck Iranian cities including Bandar Abbas and Isfahan for a 10th consecutive night; Iran retaliated against US assets in Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait, with at least 17 US service members reported killed.
Yemen's Houthis announced a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia; analysts warn that closing the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait could cut global oil supply by roughly 7%.
Khalil al-Hayya was selected to lead Hamas, replacing Yahya Sinwar, who was killed in October 2024.
President Daniel Ortega declared the country will hold no further elections, deepening its authoritarian turn.
The Democratic Republic of Congo's Ebola outbreak has now claimed about 930 lives from more than 2,300 confirmed cases, spreading faster than any prior outbreak.

sources: The Motley Fool; Yahoo Finance; TheStreet; Reuters/Investing.com; CNBC; Fortune; GV Wire; Democracy Now

label: Tue · Jul 21
standfirst: Wall Street closed the best bank-earnings season in history even as fresh U.S. strikes on Iran and a Houthi blockade threat rattled markets. The stories that matter most to you, below.
marketsNote: Roughly 86% of S&P 500 companies have beaten expectations so far this season. U.S. gasoline averaged $3.99 a gallon amid the Iran conflict.

lead.headline: Big banks post the strongest quarter in history — JPMorgan books the largest profit ever
body: All five major U.S. banks blew past estimates in the second quarter, powered by a trading boom and blockbuster deal activity — most notably the $85.7 billion SpaceX IPO, which alone generated roughly $500 million in underwriting fees split across the group. JPMorgan led with $21.2 billion in net income, the single largest quarterly profit in U.S. banking history, on revenue of $58.0 billion, up 27%.

CEO Jamie Dimon still struck a cautious note, warning of geopolitical instability, persistent inflation, and swelling sovereign debt. Goldman Sachs delivered the biggest surprise — earnings of $20.98 a share against a $14.47 estimate. Wells Fargo reported its first full quarter free of the Fed's multi-year asset cap.

banking.text: Congress enacted the bipartisan 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act. Title IX delivers community-banking reforms: expanded reciprocal deposit exceptions, a two-year capital phase-in for de novo banks, and 18-month exam cycles for banks up to $6 billion in assets.

JPMorgan, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and PNC reportedly held preliminary talks to acquire Fiserv's STAR debit network (115 million cardholders) — a way to sidestep Durbin interchange caps, but expect heavy scrutiny.

banking.lead: The Fed launches a sweeping review.
banking.text: Chair Kevin Warsh named 15 outside advisers across five task forces spanning communications, the balance sheet, and inflation frameworks. Panelists include Marc Andreessen and Raghuram Rajan; recommendations due by year-end.

banking.lead: Circle, Sony, and Klarna go bank.
banking.text: Circle won approval for a national trust bank offering digital-asset custody. Sony gained conditional approval for a trust bank planning dollar-backed stablecoins, and Klarna applied for a Utah industrial bank charter.
A federal judge temporarily blocked the bureau's workforce reduction until 60 days after a Senate-confirmed director takes office.
SWIFT launched a pilot with 17 global banks, testing 24/7 cross-border payments using tokenized deposits.

watch.title: The Fed's next move
watch.text: The FOMC meets July 28–29 with rates held at 3.50%–3.75%. Inflation is running hot — PCE at 3.6% and CPI up to 4.2% in May — and the room is split: nine of 18 officials penciled in at least one 2026 hike in June, while others still see cuts. No new projections accompany this meeting, so the statement language is the tell.

world.text: U.S. Central Command carried out a ninth consecutive night of strikes on Iranian military targets under Operation Epic Fury. Three American service members were killed in recent days. Yemen's Houthis declared a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia.
Russia launched one of its largest ballistic-missile barrages against Ukraine since the 2022 invasion began.
Venezuela's twin-earthquake death toll rose to 5,208. A 7.3-magnitude quake struck Mexico's southern coast; a 5.5 quake in Peru's Andes killed at least six.
Spain edged Argentina 1–0 in the final, with President Trump, Canada's PM Carney, and Mexico's President Sheinbaum in attendance.
Tesla, Alphabet, and Intel headline the coming week.

sources: CNBC & TheStreet market recaps; TechTimes / Intellectia Q2 bank earnings; Bankeration Banking Roundup; Forbes Fed Meeting Tracker; Everything Briefing global roundup.
